candidate_problems.text: How can we operationally distinguish substrate-limited from structure-limited epistemic progress in AI systems? The conjecture hinges on a phase shift where more compute stops being the main bottleneck and higher-level inquiry structure becomes dominant, but it does not specify measurable criteria, diagnostics, or experimental designs for identifying that boundary across domains.

Is computational irreducibility actually the right characterization of open-ended epistemic search, or are there important classes of inquiry where meta-level structure can create genuine shortcuts rather than merely better allocation of brute-force search? This is a central unresolved tension because if substantial compressions of conjecture-criticism loops are possible, the conjecture overstates the limits of scaling.

What concrete institutional or algorithmic structures best implement error-correcting epistemic dynamics in AI without causing premature convergence, excessive criticism costs, or loss of exploratory diversity? The conjecture points to mechanisms like criticism memory, adversarial testing, and promotion by refutation, but leaves open the design tradeoffs and feedback loops that determine whether such structures improve knowledge growth in practice.
